sections.text: Small language models (SLMs) have emerged as efficient alternatives to large language mod- els  for  task-specific  applications. However, they are often employed in high-volume, low- latency settings, where efficiency is crucial.  We propose TASC , Task-Adaptive Sequence Com- pression,  a  framework  for  SLM  acceleration comprising two use-cases:  When performing SLM fine-tuning, we propose TASC-ft , which iteratively  enriches  the  tokenizer  vocabulary with high-frequency output n -grams and then fine-tunes the model to utilize the expanded vo- cabulary.  Next, we propose an inference-time method,  termed  TASC-spec .  TASC-spec  is  a lightweight, training-free speculative decoding method that constructs an n -gram draft model from  the  task’s  output  corpus,  mixing  task and  context  n -gram  information.  TASC-spec avoids any additional training, while avoiding draft-target vocabulary alignment constraints. We demonstrate the effectiveness of both meth- ods across multiple low output-variability gen- eration tasks.  Our methods show consistent im- provements in inference efficiency while main- taining task performance.

Small language models (SLMs) are lightweight fast models that recently rose as a viable alternative to large language models (LLMs) ( Belcak et al. , ; Subramanian et al. , ).  SLMs are often distilled versions  of  LLMs  that  pose  specific  capabilities, with  the  intention  to  operate  as  a  part  of  a  multi- agent system ( Lee et al. , ; Sharma and Mehta , ) or in highly memory-bound high-latency set- tings, such as on-device deployment ( Kementched- jhieva and Chalkidis , ; Kandala et al. , ; Lu  et  al. ,  ).  SLMs,  while  lighter  than  their larger  counterparts,  have  demonstrated  compara- ble performance when fine-tuned (FT) on specific tasks,  from  text  classification  ( Luo  et  al. ,  2023a ; * Work was done during an internship at Amazon. Sun et al. , 2023a ; Sakai and Lam , ) and sum- marization  ( Xu  et  al. ,  ;  Bailly  et  al. ,  ), to question-answering (QA) ( Pappas et al. , ; Kweon et al. , ) and code generation ( Luo et al. , 2023b ; Pinnaparaju et al. , ). We  argue  that  tasks  naturally  suited  for  SLM FT also often exhibit lower output variability com- pared to open-ended multi-purpose text generation. This manifests in a decrease of the n -gram empir- ical  entropy  in  the  dataset  of  expected  task  out- puts.  That is, expected output texts tend to contain more n -grams that repeat across different samples. These  n -grams  often  represent  common  phrases and terms in the task’s expected outputs.  We ana- lyze this phenomenon through the lenses of typi- cality ( Cover , , Chapter 2). To accelerate LLMs, most prior work focus on either decreasing memory requirements or accel- erating input processing, with the purpose of han- dling  longer  contexts.  Popular  methods  in  these direction are model quantization ( Lang et al. , ; Lin et al. , ),  KV caching ( Pope et al. , ; Li et al. , 2024a ) and attention optimization ( Dao et al. , ; Kwon et al. , ).  On the contrary, in this work we follow the low output variability, com- mon in FT SLMs, and focus on the model’s outputs. Specifically, we view efficiency in terms of decreas- ing the total number of model auto-regressive steps required to generate a response from a given task input, which is a known bottleneck in autoregres- sive generation ( OpenAI , ).  To that end, our framework  comprising  two  solutions,  which  we term Task-Adaptive Sequence Compression ( TASC ). Each solution targets a different operational SLM use-case.  Figure outlines TASC ’s pipeline. First, when task-specific fine-tuning of the SLM is  performed,  we  propose  TASC-ft ,  a  tokenizer augmentation procedure that explicitly leverages the task-induced concentration of output n -grams. Given a train corpus of expected task outputs, we identify high-frequency n -grams whose merge into

TASC-ft (upper) augments the model’s tokenizer with n -gram tokens  and  trains  their  embeddings  during  the  model’s  fine-tune.  TASC-spec  (lower)  accelerates  the  model  in inference-time using an n -gram model drafter, providing significant speedup with no additional training. single  tokens  yields  the  largest  reduction  in  ex- pected output length across the corpus.  We itera- tively enrich the model’s tokenizer with such task- specific tokens using a byte pair encoding (BPE) inspired scheme, and fine-tune the SLM to properly utilize  the  expanded  vocabulary.  This  procedure reduces the number of decoding steps required to generate typical task outputs, resulting in substan- tial  latency  improvements  while  preserving  task performance. Second,  when  model  fine-tuning  is  not  per- formed (e.g., when using off-the-shelf fine-tuned SLMs  for  rapid  development)  we  propose  a training-free inference-time acceleration method. Inspired by speculative decoding ( Leviathan et al. , ),  we  construct  a  lightweight  n -gram  draft model directly from the task’s output corpus.  The n -gram drafter generates sequence predictions at a  negligible  cost,  which  are  then  verified  by  the target model in a single forward pass.  To balance global task structure with instance-specific informa- tion, we combine global corpus-derived n -grams with local prompt-derived information into a uni- fied draft model, which we term TASC-spec .  This approach yields meaningful speedups without ad- ditional training.  Furthermore, while most existing methods require both the target and draft to share the  same  vocabulary  –  limiting  the  cross-model applicability of speculative decoding – TASC-spec automatically adapts to the target’s vocabulary. Our main contributions are as follows: 1.  We characterize the set of generation tasks by the  relation  between  their  input  vs.  output variability, which we then formalize through the information-theoretic perspective of typi- cal n -grams. 2.  We  propose  TASC-ft ,  an  algorithm  that  en- riches the SLM’s tokenizer vocabulary with task-specific  n -grams. TASC-ft  integrates seamlessly into parameter-efficient FT, achiev- ing up to × . reductions in model runtime while preserving generation quality. 3.  We show that the token distribution’s 2-Rényi entropy can act as a predictive measure of fine- tuned model acceleration under TASC-ft . 4.  We  introduce  TASC-spec ,  a  lightweight, training-free  speculative  decoding
that  uses  n -gram  draft  models.  TASC-spec exhibits  inference  speedups  of  up  to  × . across multiple generation tasks.
Tokenizer  augmentation  Popularized  by  ( Hong et al. , ; Sachidananda et al. , ), modifica- tion of the tokenizer’s vocabulary had been recently adopted  as  a

for  improving  the  model’s domain-adaptation abilities and sequence compres- sion  capabilities.  ( Gee  et  al. ,  )  shows  that adding the top- K  most frequent n -grams improved input sequence compression for BERT-like models and ( Schmidt et al. , ; Liu et al. , ) goes be- yond pre-tokenization boundaries to include bigger token  merges.  The  most  relevant  work  is  Adap- tiVocab  ( Nakash  et  al. ,  ),  which  proposes  a domain-based vocabulary adaptation method.  Do- main adaptation targets the input corpus, thus re- quiring a significant modification of the tokenizer vocabulary. adds  a small  number  of  task-specific  tokens,  capturing most of the relevant variability without sacrificing generality or requiring token removal, and explic- itly targeting inference efficiency rather than broad domain coverage. Speculative Decoding: Popularized by ( Leviathan  et  al. ,  ;  Chen  et  al. ,  ),  spec- ulative decoding is nowadays a common method for  acceleration  of  LLMs  using  a  fast  auxiliary draft model.  The authors of ( Fu et al. , ) study draft parallel decoding using Jacobi iterations.  In ( Cai et al. , ), a multi-head drafter is proposed, where  each  head  learns  to  predict  a  different lookahead step.  The authors of ( Li et al. , 2024b ) transform  the  speculative  decoding  problem  to the feature space via EAGLE. ( Sun et al. , 2023b ) shows  that  speculative  decoding  is  theoretically optimal  using  optimal  transport  theory. Most related  work  in  this  field  is  n -grammys  ( Stewart et al. , ), in which a simple bigram model that assigns  each  token  its  most  probable  next  token under  the  given  model. However,  n -grammys is  not  task-specific, trading  generality  with performance. n -Grams for LLMs:  Despite the dominance of transformer-based models, n -gram statistics remain relevant  for  efficiency,  adaptation,  and  analysis. Early works combined neural models with external n -gram LMs to inject domain-specific biases with- out retraining ( Gulcehre et al. , ), while later retrieval-based methods generalized n -gram mem- orization  through  nearest-neighbor  interpolation ( Khandelwal  et  al. ,  ).  In  a  related  direction, Infini-grams ( Liu et al. , ) leverage unbounded- length n -gram statistics to analyze and approximate LLM  next-token  behavior.  In  ( Diao  et  al. ,  ) n -gram information is incorporated as explicit fea- tures or auxiliary representations.  Recent analyses further suggest that LLMs often behave similarly to high-order n -gram models over substantial por- tions of the token distribution ( Nguyen , ; Varre et al. , ).

While SLMs are capable of open-ended generation, many  practical  applications  rely  on  constrained generation tasks .  In these scenarios, the output is not free-form but strictly bound by a predefined la- bel set, structural schema, or other specific require- ments.  In this section, we formalize and quantify this observation through an information-theoretic perspective. 3.1 Task-Induced n -Grams Concentration Consider some generation task τ  that has a repre- sentative corpus, i.e., a collection of input texts and corresponding expected outputs.  We denote the set of expected outputs for a given task τ  by E τ .  For some finite n , we consider the empirical output n - gram distribution based on the n -gram frequencies in E τ .   This is a conditional distribution, where the conditioning is on τ .  For example, in POS-tagging, expected output are sequences of discrete part-of- speech labels (e.g., DET , NOUN , VERB ), drawn from a  small,  fixed  tag  set.  Here,  the  n -gram  distribu- tion is significantly skewed towards specific label As  we  are  yet  to  consider  a  specific  tokenizer,  we  can, without loss of generality, consider word n -grams. sequences, unlike the heavier-tailed distribution of open-ended text. Formally,  the  skewed  n -gram  distribution  ex- hibits reduced entropy: H ( T  n | T = τ ) ≪ H ( T  n ) , (1) where  T  n  ≜ ( T , . . . T n )  is  the  n -gram  random variable and H ( T  n ) is its entropy, unconditioned on any specific τ .  Following the information the- oretic notion of typicality ( Shannon , ; Cover , ), the size of the set containing most probable task output n -grams, denoted A n,τ , is determined by this conditional entropy:  A n,τ  ≈ exp ( H ( T n | T = τ )) . (2) We  call  A n, t  the  typical  set  of  output  n -grams given  generation  task  τ .  A n, t  is  potentially  ex- ponentially smaller than the full space of n -grams. Consequently,  a relatively small number of high- probability n -grams dominate the task-conditioned distribution. We demonstrate this phenomenon across a range of  tasks,  using  word  bigrams  for  simplicity.  For each dataset, we measure the bigram entropy and the number of unique bigrams that attain 80% of the bigram CDF. Both metrics serve as proxies for the size and dominance of the typical set of bigrams in E τ .  As shown in Table , some generation tasks result in a small set of bigrams that cover a large portion  of  the  bigram  distribution.  We  examine output variability in a comparative manner against the  inputs,  assuming  inputs  are  provided  in  free- form natural language. Notably, the tasks at hand exist along a spectrum, defined  in  terms  of  the  input  vs.  output  variabil- ity.  To  capture  this,  we  measure  the  difference in  entropy  and  CDF  coverage  ratio  between  the input  and  output  bigram  distributions.  In  highly constrained tasks, such as sentiment analysis, the typical  set  is  very  small,  implying  that  most  of the output distribution is concentrated on a small set.  Conversely, in tasks such as creative writing, outputs exhibit higher variability than inputs. The  comparative  quantification  distinguishes tasks  from  domains ;  while  fixing  a  domain  may reduce  variability,  this  reduction  applies  to  both inputs and outputs, and not necessarily in the same Formally, we can take a uniform prior over possible tasks and define H ( T  n ) ≜ |T | − P τ ∈T  H ( T n | T = τ ) . We  make  this  assumption  as  often  those  may  contain human-written texts. Figure 2: Effect of added tokens on the tokenized output texts in the EUR-LEX dataset, n max  = 4 . magnitude  as  outputs  may  have  their  unique  em- phasis  or  style.  In  contrast,  as  shown  in  Table  , fixing a task primarily affects the output distribu- tion. Consequently, the dominant factor influencing the difference in input vs.  output variability is the type  of  task,  rather  than  the  domain.  For  exam- ple, Yes/No QA exhibits a significant reduction in output variability compared to summarization, al- though both tasks operate within the medical texts domain.  In what follows, we propose TASC , which aims to accelerate SLMs whose target is low output variability  tasks  (except  for  simple  classification tasks that have extremely low variability and don’t require acceleration). As we later explain, each task is aimed at a different use-case and aims to address a different regime of the variability spectrum.

When  fine-tuning  on  the  task  at  hand  is  both  de- sired  and  feasible,  we  extend  the  standard  fine- tuning  procedure  with  a  tokenizer  preprocessing step.  Specifically,  we define a score for merging an n -gram of tokens into a single new token.  The score is used in a BPE-inspired algorithm to itera- tively expand the tokenizer vocabulary with high- scoring n -grams.  The new n -gram token embed- dings are optimized alongside the fine tuning pro- cedure. 4.1 Enriching the Tokenizer Let V base denote the model’s vocabulary and let E τ be the outputs of the train set.  We treat it as a rep- resentative corpus of the task’s outputs distribution, consisting of token sequences over V base .  Notably, E τ  contains only expected outputs, as our goal is to  reduce  decoding-time  latency  rather  than  pre- fill cost.  We propose a BPE-inspired scheme  that See Appendix A for information on BPE. iteratively  adds  token  n -grams  to  V base  with  the purpose of reducing the expected output length in E τ .  To identify potential token n -gram candidates, we define the merge reward of each token n -gram t n  by its expected contribution to sequence-length reduction: f merge ( t n ) ≜ freq ( t n ) · ( n − 1) , (3) where freq ( t n ) is the n -gram’s frequency in E τ  and the  factor  ( n  − 1)  corresponds  to  the  net  reduc- tion in token count, if we were to merge a single occurrence of t n  into a single token. While  the  merge  reward  captures  expected sequence-length  reduction,  introducing  a  new  n - gram token can bias greedy decoding by collapsing multiple plausible continuations into a single dom- inant path,  suppressing alternatives whose prefix collides with the new token’s suffix.  For instance, adding the bigram (’ in ’, ’ the ’) as a new token may lead the model to favor this continuation over in- dividually less frequent but collectively probable alternatives  like  ’ therapy ’  or  ’ theorem ’,  which share the prefix ’ the ’.  To mitigate this effect, we propose the Prefix Collision Score (PCS), which measures  the  probability  that  the  suffix  of  an  n - gram collides with prefixes of existing tokens.  For- mally,  for  an  n -gram  t n ,  let  str ( t n )  be  its  string representation.  We define: PCS ( t n ) = P [ t ′ ∈V str ( t n ) is a prefix of ( str ( t ′ ) ! for t ′  ∈V i over E τ .  We therefore add t n  to V i only if PCS ( t n ) falls below a prescribed threshold. Starting from V  =  V base , at each step i we ex- tend V i with the element that maximizes f merge ( t n ) and  that  does  not  pass  the  PCS  threshold.  This repeats  until  a  predefined  vocabulary  budget  is reached or a predetermined number of iterations are performed. We refer to this method as TASC-ft and outline its steps in Algorithm .  Figure demon- strates the effect of TASC-ft on EUR-LEX , a mas- sive  multi-label  classification  dataset  ( Chalkidis et al. , ), in which every text has up to labels out of K  classes.  We evaluate the average output length  and  measure  the  output  compression  rate over  the  tokenized  validation  set.  Consequently, with  only   new  tokens  we  obtain  × .  re- duction  in  the  average  output  length  and  × . increase in the bytes-per token metric. We  find  additional  motivation  in  the  uniform information distribution (UID) hypothesis ( Jaeger Algorithm 1 TASC-ft :  Tokenizer Enrichment Require:  E τ , V base , token budget M , n max , PCS threshold α PCS 1:  Initialize i = 0 , usedNgrams = set () 2:  Set V i  = V base 3:  while i < M  do 4: Ngrams = collectNgrams ( V i , n max ) 5: Calculate f merge for t n  ∈ Ngrams 6: Take t ⋆ ∈ arg max t n ∈V n i  f merge ( t n ) 7: if PCS ( t ⋆ ) < α PCS then 8: V i  ←V i ∪ ( t ⋆ ) 9: i ← i + 1 10: end if 11: usedNgrams ← t ⋆ 12:  end while 13:  return Enriched tokenizer vocabulary V and Levy , ), which posits that efficient linguis- tic  representations  tend  to  distribute  information evenly across tokens, leading to approximately uni- form  per-token  surprisal  along  sequences.  This principle motivates our procedure.  By collapsing typical high-probability n -grams into single tokens, the  induced  token  distribution  over  the  enriched vocabulary becomes less peaked, increasing the to- ken distribution entropy and expanding the typical set.  For example, on the EUR-LEX dataset, enrich- ing the tokenizer with task-specific n -grams increases the normalized entropy of the empirical output token distribution from . to . (see
